title: OUT_FULL_16 and OUT_NO_ORIENT both equal 8, making them indistinguishable in any bitmask test
description: Line 551 defines `OUT_FULL_16 = 8` and line 556 defines `OUT_NO_ORIENT = 8`. Every bitmask test (`output_flags & OUT_FULL_16 != 0`) is identical to (`output_flags & OUT_NO_ORIENT != 0`), so callers cannot request full-16 output without also skipping orientation, or vice versa. The public API doc at line 953 says 'Pass 7 for classic. For M3 16-bit big levels pass e.g. 15 (7|8)', confirming callers expect flag 8 to mean OUT_FULL_16, but process_orf_impl (line 743) and process_dng_impl (line 1825) both check `OUT_FULL_16` for the 16-bit buffer while the OUT_NO_ORIENT guard at line 801 checks the same bit, silently also skipping orientation for every OUT_FULL_16 caller.
evidence: Verified at src/lib.rs:551 and 556 — both constants are literally `8u32`. At line 800 `(output_flags & OUT_NO_ORIENT) != 0` fires whenever OUT_FULL_16 is set, unconditionally skipping orientation for every M3 caller who passes flags 8 or 15.

title: Flipflop bench mutates rgb16 in-place so subsequent flip rounds blend already-blended data, not a fresh baseline
description: The flip branch (rounds 0, 2, 4, 6) applies the NR blend `o + (b - o) * s` in-place to `rgb16` (line 490). Each subsequent flip round therefore starts from the output of the previous round rather than fresh source data. After 4 flip iterations the array has converged toward `blurred` by factor `(1 - s*(1 - s*(1 - s*(1 - s))))`, not `s`. The median across 4 such rounds measures diverging work and will give artificially low timings for later rounds as the data converges. To get a fair per-call benchmark, `rgb16` must be restored to its original state before each timed invocation.
evidence: Verified at raw_decode_bench.rs:490 — `rgb16[i] = ...` writes back the blended value in-place. Both the flip (line 490) and flop (line 502) branches mutate `rgb16`, so each round operates on previously-processed data rather than the original synthetic values.

title: meanLuma is divided by 256 but luma values reach 65025, producing an unnormalized result in [0, ~254] instead of [0, 1]
description: In `fs_to_js` the luma accumulation uses `l = 54*r + 183*g + 18*b` (weights sum to 255), so per-pixel luma spans 0..=65025 for 8-bit RGBA. `mean` is the average of these per-pixel values. Line 2375 then computes `mean / 256.0`. The resulting `meanLuma` field will lie in [0, ~254] rather than [0, 1] as callers likely expect for a normalized mean luma. `lumaVariance` (line 2362) divides by 65536, which is also inconsistent with the luma domain. The correct normalization for `meanLuma` should be `mean / 65280.0` (the maximum, 255*255) or equivalently divide the per-pixel luma first by 255*255 before summing.
evidence: The JS reference at web/jxl-progressive-frame-stats.js:157 explicitly documents 'meanLuma is the per-channel-weighted mean rescaled to ~0..255' and applies the same `/ 256` divisor (line 159: `meanLuma: meanInt / 256`). The tools/frame-stats-flipflop.mjs also uses `mean / 256`. The range [0, ~254] is intentional and matches the JS contract; callers do not expect [0, 1].

title: fs_core_simd tail path resets a_min/a_max to sentinel only after a px==0 guard that is never true at that point
description: In `fs_core_simd` (wasm32 version), the `if px == 0 { a_min = 255; a_max = 0; }` guard at line 2593 is placed after the tail loop. However by that point a_min and a_max have already been set from the SIMD vmin/vmax extraction immediately above (lines 2557-2570). If px==0 then chunks==0 so vmin stays at the initialized splat(255) and vmax stays at splat(0), and extracting lane 3 from these gives 255 and 0 respectively, so the guard is correct in result — but it is doing redundant work. In the SIMD accumulator path (chunks > 0) the tail loop further updates a_min/a_max. The real issue is that if px==0 the vmin/vmax extraction loop at lines 2558-2570 still runs (extracting from splat(255)/splat(0)), setting a_min=255, a_max=0, then the tail loop skips (chunks*4==px==0), then the guard reassigns the same values. This is harmless dead work but confirms the guard is a copy-paste from `fs_core_fast` that was not reconciled with the SIMD init path.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2557-2593. When px==0, vmin/vmax retain their splat(255)/splat(0) initializations, lane extraction gives a_min=255 and a_max=0, and the tail loop runs 0 iterations. The guard at line 2593 then redundantly reassigns the same values — dead code that poses no correctness risk.

title: DNG/CR2 ProcessResult uses exposure_den=1 / fnumber_den=1 / focal_length_den=1 as absent-sentinel, but ORF path uses 0 (breaking JS consumers that check den==0)
description: In `process_dng_impl` (and therefore `process_cr2_impl` via the shared path) at lines 1930-1935, missing rational EXIF values are filled with numerator=0 / denominator=1. The ORF path (`process_orf_impl`, lines 842-848) uses denominator=0 as the absent sentinel. The code comment on line 841 documents '0/0 as absent-sentinel (JS checks den==0 before dividing)'. JS consumers using `result.exposure_den === 0` to detect absent exposure will incorrectly treat DNG/CR2 images as having exposure 0/1 = 0 rather than absent, and may display '0 s' instead of no exposure value. The correct sentinel for DNG/CR2 should also be 0.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:841-848 (ORF: den=0 sentinel with explicit comment) and lib.rs:1929-1935 (DNG: exposure_den=1, fnumber_den=1, focal_length_den=1). The sentinel mismatch is real and will cause JS consumers that gate on `den === 0` to compute 0/1=0 for absent DNG EXIF fields instead of skipping display.

title: time_fn median index ms[runs/2] is the upper-middle element for even runs counts, not the true median
description: For `runs = 5`, `runs/2 = 2` (integer division), which is the middle element of a 0-indexed 5-element sorted array — correct. However the function is not restricted to odd run counts. For an even number of runs (e.g., if a caller passes `runs=4`), `ms[runs/2]` is `ms[2]` which is the upper-middle element; the true median of 4 elements is `(ms[1]+ms[2])/2`. The main() call uses `runs=5` so this does not cause a bug in practice, but the generality is broken and the `med` label in the output would be misleading for even values.
evidence: Verified in blur_bench.rs — `ms[runs / 2]` is the upper-middle element for even `runs`. In practice `runs=5` is always passed so this is harmless, but the implementation is generically wrong and the label 'med' would be misleading for any even run count.

title: No compile-time or runtime assertion that output flag constants are distinct
description: The OUT_* constants (lines 548-556) are hand-assigned u32 values. The current collision between OUT_FULL_16 and OUT_NO_ORIENT (both = 8) was silently accepted by the compiler. A `const _: () = assert!(OUT_FULL_16 != OUT_NO_ORIENT && OUT_FULL_RGB8 != OUT_LIGHTBOX ...);` guard evaluated at compile time would catch any future or existing collision at zero runtime cost. This is a structural gap that allowed the existing HIGH severity bug to be undetected.
evidence: No compile-time assert exists for flag distinctness, as proven by the logic-1 collision being present in shipping code. A `const _: () = assert!(...)` block at the constant site would have prevented the logic-1 HIGH bug from being introduced.

title: OUT_FULL_16 and OUT_NO_ORIENT share the same bitmask value (8), silently suppressing orientation when full-16 output is requested
description: Both `OUT_FULL_16` (line 551) and `OUT_NO_ORIENT` (line 556) are defined as `8u32`. Any caller that passes `output_flags | OUT_FULL_16` (e.g. 7|8 = 15 for 'M3 full-16') will also have the `OUT_NO_ORIENT` bit set, causing `process_orf_impl` and `process_dng_impl` to skip `apply_orientation` on the full RGB8 output. The callers and docstring for `process_orf_with_flags` explicitly advise passing 15 for M3 full-16, which means all M3 full-16 outputs are silently unrotated. This is a logic error introduced by reusing a bit position for two unrelated features.
evidence: Duplicate of logic-1 / contracts-1 / errors-5. Verified: both constants equal 8 at lib.rs:551 and 556; orientation skip fires unconditionally on any M3 full-16 call.

title: now_ms() re-executes the full JS-global lookup on every call from rayon worker threads when built with parallel-wasm
description: The `now_ms` function caches a `web_sys::Performance` object in a `thread_local! { OnceCell<Performance> }`. On rayon worker threads (spawned by wasm-bindgen-rayon), `web_sys::window()` returns `None` and the `WorkerGlobalScope::dyn_into` also fails, so `perf` is always `None`. Because `cell.set(perf)` is only called when `perf` is `Some`, the `OnceCell` for each rayon thread stays empty forever. Every timing call from a rayon context re-runs the failed DOM lookups, adding unnecessary JS-FFI overhead per timing sample across all parallel tone/demosaic work that uses `now_ms`. The correct fix is to set the cell to a sentinel or call `Date::now()` directly from rayon contexts without attempting the DOM lookup.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:311-335: `cell.set()` is only called in the `Some(perf)` branch; when both window() and WorkerGlobalScope lookups fail (rayon worker context), the fallback to `Date::now()` runs without setting the cell. Each subsequent call repeats the two failed DOM lookups before reaching the fallback.

title: Unsafe raw pointer advance in v_pass_tiled lacks a bounds check for the right-edge partial tile, risking out-of-bounds reads on malformed synthetic data
description: The `v_pass_tiled` function computes `sp = src.as_ptr().add(row + x0 * 3)` and then advances it `tile * 3` times via unchecked `sp.add(1)`. The invariant relies on `x1 = (x0 + TILE).min(w)` and `tile = x1 - x0`, so the maximum pointer position reached is `row + (x0 + tile) * 3 - 1`. For the last tile at the right edge, `x0 + tile == w`, so the last read is `src[row + w*3 - 1]`, which is within bounds as long as `src.len() >= (yi * w + w) * 3`. This holds for valid calls but is not checked inside the unsafe block. The safety comment says 'SAFETY: tile, x0, yi validated by caller contract + clamp; bench synthetic data,' but there is no explicit assertion. If ever called with a `src` slice shorter than `h * w * 3` this is UB. Bench-only context mitigates severity.
evidence: Verified at blur_bench.rs:124-134. The SAFETY comment cites clamp+caller contract but does not prove `src.len() >= h*w*3`. The bench always constructs src of exactly `w*h*3` elements so no actual UB occurs, but the unsafe block lacks a formal precondition proof that would catch future caller errors.

title: bench_perf_flipflop mutates rgb16 in-place on flip rounds, meaning flop rounds measure a different (already-processed) input than the original synthetic data
description: The flip branch (serial while-loop, lines 486-493) modifies `rgb16` in place by blending it with `blurred`. After round 0 (flip), `rgb16` no longer contains the original synthetic values; rounds 2, 4, 6 (the remaining flips) operate on already-blended data, and rounds 1, 3, 5, 7 (flops) operate on partially-blended data from prior flips. The benchmark is intended to compare the same operation at serial vs parallel speed, but the input diverges across rounds, making median timings slightly less representative. It also means the flip-flop result is not idempotent across multiple benchmark runs in the same process (e.g. if `bench_perf_flipflop` were called twice). Low severity because it is a benchmark-only binary and the timing difference introduced is small.
evidence: Duplicate of logic-2. Verified: both flip (line 490) and flop (line 502) overwrite rgb16 in-place, meaning each round operates on previously-processed data.

title: Long-running WASM pipeline functions (decode_orf_raw, decode_dng_raw) have no cancellation path for the parallel-wasm build
description: When built with `parallel-wasm` and rayon, the demosaic and tone stages can spawn many rayon threads. There is no mechanism to cancel an in-progress decode if the JS caller navigates away or calls another decode. The only isolation is that each wasm-bindgen export is synchronous and JS is single-threaded, but rayon worker threads may still be running decompress or demosaic work from a prior call when the next export is invoked. Adding a cooperative cancellation flag (e.g. an `AtomicBool` checked between rayon chunks) would prevent wasted CPU when the caller has already moved on.
evidence: WASM exports are synchronous from JS's perspective — the JS main thread is blocked for the duration. Rayon worker threads finish before the export returns. There is no concurrent invocation scenario in a single-threaded JS context; this is an architectural concern (design tradeoff) rather than a concrete bug with a failure scenario.

title: fstats_copy computes px = width * height without overflow guard, then indexes into pixels up to px*4 bytes
description: The exported WASM function `fstats_copy(pixels, width, height)` computes `px = width * height` using plain Rust `usize` multiplication (line 2488). On wasm32 `usize` is 32 bits; a caller passing width=65536 and height=65536 overflows to px=0, making the loop a no-op. More dangerously, values like width=1<<16, height=1<<15 give px=0x8000_0000 which as `px*4` wraps to 0 — but the loop bounds come from `px` so the actual read comes from `pixels.len()` guard inside `fs_core_scalar`. The real risk is that `fs_core_scalar` indexes `d[i]` through `d[i+3]` for `i` up to `(px-1)*4`; if `pixels.len() < px*4` due to the overflow, Rust's slice indexing will panic (not UB), but the width/height values are fully JS-controlled with no validation. Compare with `frame_stats` (line 2819) which uses `width.saturating_mul(height)`. This inconsistency means `fstats_copy` does not have the same overflow protection.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2487-2490 — `px = width * height` with no overflow check, while the equivalent `frame_stats` at line 2819 uses `width.saturating_mul(height)`. On wasm32 an overflow to a small px causes fs_core_scalar to index past pixels.len(), resulting in a Rust panic (not silent UB, but still a crash).

category: unsafe memory access / potential out-of-bounds read
title: fs_core_simd uses unsafe v128_load without alignment or bounds comment; relies on caller contract that px*4 <= d.len()
description: Both `fs_core_simd` (line 2522) and `fs_core_simd_exact` (line 2639) use `unsafe { v128_load(d.as_ptr().add(i) as *const v128) }` where `i = c * 16`. The load reads 16 bytes; the loop runs for `chunks = px / 4` iterations. If any caller passes a slice shorter than `px * 4` bytes the load reads past the end of the slice — undefined behavior in Rust unsafe. In practice the two callers are `fstats_scalar` / `fstats_simd` (which use the resident buffer filled to `w*h*4`) and the production `frame_stats` (which guards with `limit = pixels.len().min(expected)` and takes the truncated path when `limit != expected`). However there is no in-function assert; the safety relies purely on call-site discipline. A future caller or refactor that passes a shorter-than-expected slice would silently cause UB.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2519-2522. There is no SAFETY comment or debug_assert! inside fs_core_simd verifying `d.len() >= px * 4`. The three callers currently uphold this invariant, but there is no in-function guard. A future refactor passing a mismatched slice would produce real UB (out-of-bounds v128_load).

title: PerceptualComparer::new computes n = width * height without overflow guard before allocating n*4 bytes
description: The WASM-exported `PerceptualComparer::new(ref_rgba, width, height)` computes `let n = width * height` (line 2265) then allocates `vec![0u8; n * 4]` for the scratch buffer. On wasm32, both multiplications are unchecked 32-bit operations. A caller passing width=0x8000_0000 and height=2 would compute n=0 (overflow) and allocate an empty scratch buffer. Subsequent `all_at(len)` calls with a non-zero len would then slice an empty buffer, panicking. More critically, `input_ptr(len)` would resize to `len` so the scratch grows correctly — but the initial allocation and the inner `PerceptualCore::new(ref_rgba, width, height, ...)` may also perform unchecked arithmetic on the attacker-controlled dimensions. The ref_rgba length is not cross-checked against width*height before constructing PerceptualCore.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2264-2267. `let n = width * height` is unchecked; `vec![0u8; n * 4]` has another unchecked multiply. No length cross-check on ref_rgba. Every other public WASM entry point (downscale_rgba, etc.) validates dimensions first. This is also covered by errors-1 and errors-10.

category: JSON injection via unescaped string fields
title: rows_to_json interpolates file name and decode_strategy strings into JSON without escaping quotes or control characters
description: The manual JSON serializer in `rows_to_json` interpolates `row.file` (line 206) and `row.decode_strategy` (line 222) directly into JSON string literals using `format!("..."{}")`. The only sanitization is replacing backslash with forward-slash in `row.file`. A filename containing a double-quote character — which is legal on Linux/macOS filesystems — would produce malformed JSON (e.g. `{"file": "foo"bar.orf", ...}`). Since the output file (`benchmark/results_native.json`) is consumed by downstream tools and potentially checked into source control, a crafted filename could corrupt the JSON or inject extra fields. This is a local developer tool so exploitability is limited, but the pattern is still incorrect.
evidence: Verified at raw_decode_bench.rs:206 — only backslash→slash substitution is applied, no escaping of double-quotes, newlines, or other JSON-special characters. The same issue applies to decode_strategy at line 222. A filename with a quote would break the JSON output.

category: unsafe pointer arithmetic — missing bounds proof
title: v_pass_tiled uses raw pointer arithmetic with a SAFETY comment that understates the actual precondition
description: The `// SAFETY: tile, x0, yi validated by caller contract + clamp; bench synthetic data.` comment (line 124) is misleading: the clamp only bounds `yi` to `[0, h-1]`; it does not prevent `row + x0 * 3 + tile * 3` from exceeding `src.len()`. If `w` and `h` are constructed so that `x1 = (x0 + TILE).min(w)` still causes `(yi * w + x1 - 1) * 3 + 2 >= src.len()`, the pointer reads past the allocation. In practice the benchmark always creates `src` of exactly `w * h * 3` elements and this is a developer-only binary with hardcoded synthetic data, so real exploitability is nil. The finding is that the `unsafe` block lacks a formal invariant proof and could silently become UB if caller-provided `w`, `h`, or kernel size change.
evidence: The pointer arithmetic is bounded by `tile = x1 - x0` where `x1 = (x0 + TILE).min(w)`, so the maximum offset is `row + w*3 - 1` which equals `yi*w*3 + w*3 - 1`. src is always `h*w*3` in length so `yi*w*3 + w*3 - 1 < h*w*3`. The SAFETY comment is imprecise but the bounds are actually provably safe given the construction; no real UB scenario exists in the bench binary.

title: WASM public API functions accept floating-point look parameters with no range validation before passing to the pipeline
description: The exported functions `process_orf`, `process_dng`, `process_cr2`, and `apply_look` accept floating-point parameters (exposure_ev, contrast, highlights, shadows, etc.) and `output_flags` (u32 bitmask) from JavaScript with no range validation. While `wb_r`/`wb_b` are clamped to `min(8.0)` and checked for finite/positive values, the other parameters (e.g. `exposure_ev`, `saturation`, `clarity`) are passed directly to `apply_look_params` without bounds checking. An adversarial caller could pass `f32::INFINITY`, `f32::NAN`, or extreme values that propagate through tone math and produce garbled but not-panicking output. Adding a validation layer at the WASM boundary (clamp or reject out-of-range look params) would harden against malformed callers and make the API contract explicit.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:761-781 — wb_r/wb_b are validated (finite+positive+clamped) but other look params (exposure_ev, contrast, highlights, shadows, whites, blacks, saturation, vibrance, temp, tint, texture, clarity) pass through unchecked to apply_look_params. NaN/Infinity propagation through tone math would produce garbled pixels without an error.

title: No evidence of cargo audit or dependency vulnerability scanning in the build pipeline
description: The codebase depends on wasm-bindgen, js-sys, web-sys, and (conditionally) libjxl via the jxl-ffi crate. There is no cargo-audit step visible in the repo build scripts. Adding `cargo audit` (or `cargo deny`) to CI would catch known CVEs in transitive dependencies before they reach production WASM artifacts served to browsers. This is a standard supply-chain hygiene measure for any WASM API that processes untrusted binary blobs.
code_excerpt: // No audit step found in build-parallel-wasm.ps1 or build-msvc.ps1
evidence: Cannot verify from the source code alone whether a CI pipeline with cargo-audit exists outside the visible build scripts. The finding is plausible but depends on CI configuration not present in the repo.

title: PerceptualComparer::new silently constructs with mismatched dimensions if ref_rgba length does not match width*height*4
description: The constructor accepts arbitrary `ref_rgba`, `width`, and `height` without validating that `ref_rgba.len() == width * height * 4`. If the caller passes a shorter or longer buffer the inner `PerceptualCore::new` receives garbage dimensions, potentially causing out-of-bounds reads inside the kernel. Every other public WASM entry (`downscale_rgb`, `downscale_rgba`, `LookRenderer::new`, `apply_look`, `rotate_rgb8`) performs this check and returns `Err(JsError)`. The constructor cannot return `Result` as-is, so either it should be changed to return `Result<PerceptualComparer, JsError>` or it must panic with a clear message.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2263-2268. No length check on ref_rgba against width*height*4. Also duplicated as security-3, errors-10, contracts-3. The missing validation is a real gap vs. every other public WASM constructor in this file.

title: PerceptualComparer::all_at does not validate that len <= scratch.len(), allowing out-of-bounds slice
description: If `all_at(len)` is called with `len` larger than the staging buffer (e.g. after `input_ptr` was never called, or called with a smaller value), the slice `&buf[..len]` will panic with an index-out-of-bounds. The caller controls `len` from JS. `input_ptr` grows the buffer to at least `len`, but the two calls are not atomically linked — a caller can call `input_ptr(N)`, then call `all_at(N+1)`. A simple `let len = len.min(self.scratch.len())` guard or an explicit error return is needed.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2298-2304. `&buf[..len]` will panic if len > buf.len() (Rust bounds check). A JS caller that calls all_at(N+1) after input_ptr(N) — or skips input_ptr entirely — triggers the panic. Also duplicated as contracts-4.

title: frame_stats uses saturating_mul for px then multiplies by 4 without overflow guard, producing a wrong limit on huge inputs
description: `px = width.saturating_mul(height)` correctly saturates to `usize::MAX` on overflow, but `expected = px * 4` immediately afterwards can overflow on 32-bit WASM targets where `usize` is 32 bits and a large `px` multiplied by 4 wraps. The subsequent `limit = pixels.len().min(expected)` would then compute a truncated (wrong) limit, causing `fs_core_trunc_word` to be called with an incorrect `limit` value and potentially returning corrupt stats. The fix is `let expected = px.saturating_mul(4)`.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2819-2820. `px.saturating_mul(height)` can produce up to usize::MAX=0xFFFF_FFFF on wasm32. Then `px * 4` wraps — e.g. px=0x4000_0000 gives expected=0. limit=min(pixels.len(), 0)=0, causing fs_core_trunc_word to run with limit=0 and return zero/corrupt stats for a valid large image.

title: metrics_to_js discards all Reflect::set errors with let _ =, silently producing an empty JS object on failure
description: All three `js_sys::Reflect::set` calls in `metrics_to_js` discard the `Result` with `let _ =`. If `Reflect::set` throws (e.g. the object is non-extensible, or a proxy rejects the set), the returned JsValue will be an object missing some or all fields. The caller (`PerceptualComparer::all`, `all_at`) forwards this silently to JS. A Reflect::set failure is unlikely in normal usage, but callers that depend on the fields being present will silently receive zero/undefined. For a `metrics_to_js` helper this is low risk in practice, but it is still a dropped error.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2307-2313. All three Reflect::set results are discarded with `let _ =`. A non-extensible object or proxy trap would silently produce a partial/empty JS object. Pattern is widespread in the codebase but metrics_to_js is the only place where it affects three fields at once.

title: OUT_FULL_16 and OUT_NO_ORIENT share the same bitmask value 8, silently activating both behaviours when either flag is intended
description: Constants `OUT_FULL_16` and `OUT_NO_ORIENT` are both defined as `8` (the same bit). Any caller passing flag `8` intending to request full-resolution 16-bit output will also silently skip orientation, and vice versa. The DNG path explicitly checks `output_flags & OUT_NO_ORIENT` and `output_flags & OUT_FULL_16` separately, so in practice flag 8 activates both branches simultaneously. No runtime error is produced; callers who expect only one behaviour will get the other silently. The fix is to assign distinct bits (e.g. `OUT_FULL_16 = 8`, `OUT_NO_ORIENT = 16`).
evidence: Duplicate of logic-1, concurrency-1, contracts-1. Verified: same literal value 8 at lib.rs:551 and 556.

title: decode_dng_raw pixel-count overflow check uses checked_mul but then computes w*h unchecked in the error message
description: The guard at line 1741 uses `w.checked_mul(h).unwrap_or(MAX_PIXELS + 1) > MAX_PIXELS` to detect overflow, correctly returning an error on overflow. However, the error message inside the Err branch at line 1744 computes `w * h` without a checked multiply. On 32-bit WASM targets where `usize` is 32 bits, `w * h` can itself overflow and wrap to a small number, producing a misleading error message such as "DNG: 196608 pixels exceeds 50 MP limit" when the actual product overflowed. A parallel bug exists in `decode_cr2_raw` at line 2086-2090.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:1741-1745. The guard correctly uses checked_mul, but the format string on line 1744 uses plain `w * h` which can overflow on wasm32 when checked_mul detected an overflow. The error message would display a wrapped/wrong pixel count instead of the overflowed value.

title: bench_dng uses Path::file_name().unwrap() which panics if path ends in ".."
description: At line 264, `Path::new(path).file_name().unwrap()` panics if `path` ends in `..` or is the root `/`. The same pattern appears in `bench_cr2` (line 335) and `bench_orf` (line 411). While paths passed to these functions are constructed programmatically, any directory path fed via the env vars (GOB_ROOT, P2200_ROOT) ending in a separator or `..` would cause a panic instead of a graceful skip. Using `.unwrap_or_default()` or a fallback like `path` itself is a simple improvement.
code_excerpt: let name = Path::new(path).file_name().unwrap().to_string_lossy();
evidence: Verified at raw_decode_bench.rs:264. `file_name()` returns None for paths ending in '..' or root; `.unwrap()` would panic. Paths come from env-var-rooted glob-like iteration so the risk is low in practice, but the panic is reachable with adversarial input.

title: SMALL_CROP_TIMES Mutex lock failure is silently ignored, dropping crop timing data
description: The lock acquisition `SMALL_CROP_TIMES.lock()` is matched with `if let Ok(mut v)` — a poisoned Mutex (which can happen if a thread panics while holding it) silently discards all crop timing data for that file. The same pattern appears for `LOWLEVEL_PROG_FIRSTS` at line 912. For a benchmark tool this results in missing rows in the parity summary without any diagnostic. A `.expect()` or explicit `eprintln` on the `Err` arm would surface the problem.
evidence: Verified at raw_decode_bench.rs:888-890. `if let Ok(...)` silently drops data on Mutex poison. A poisoned mutex means a prior thread panicked while holding it — an abnormal state that should be diagnosed, not silently swallowed.

title: PerceptualComparer::input_ptr accepts an unbounded len causing unbounded heap growth
description: `input_ptr` grows the scratch buffer to `len` bytes with no upper bound. A JS caller can pass `len = usize::MAX` (or any very large value) causing an OOM in the WASM linear memory. Other WASM entry points that accept buffer sizes validate them against `width*height`. This function has no such guard. Adding a check that `len <= width * height * 4` (stored at construction time) or a reasonable cap (e.g. 256 MB) would prevent a JS caller from crashing the tab.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2289-2293. `self.scratch.resize(len, 0)` with no upper bound. A JS caller passing a very large `len` triggers an unbounded heap allocation and likely an OOM panic in the WASM linear memory.

title: PerceptualComparer::new computes width*height without overflow check before allocating scratch
description: `let n = width * height` at line 2265 can overflow on 32-bit WASM if width and height are both large (e.g. both near 65535). The resulting `n` wraps and `scratch` is allocated to a much smaller (or zero) size than intended. Subsequent `input_ptr(n*4)` or `all_at(n*4)` calls will then panic or silently pass a mismatched length. Using `width.saturating_mul(height)` or a checked multiply with a cap (matching the `validate_orf_structure` limit of 50 MP) would prevent this.
evidence: Duplicate of errors-1 / security-3 / contracts-3. Verified: unchecked multiply at lib.rs:2265. Same root cause, distinct emphasis on overflow vs. length mismatch.

title: OUT_NO_ORIENT flag is not documented in any public API comment, making flag collisions with OUT_FULL_16 invisible to callers
description: The `OUT_NO_ORIENT` constant (value 8, same as `OUT_FULL_16`) is defined with a comment but never mentioned in the public doc-comments for `process_orf_with_flags`, `process_dng_with_flags`, or `process_cr2_with_flags`. Callers who read the public API docs only see flags 1/2/4/8 described for the `_with_flags` variants, and passing 8 intending to get full-res 16-bit also silently skips orientation. This is a documentation gap that increases the likelihood of the dual-meaning constant bug (errors-5) being introduced by future contributors.
evidence: Verified that OUT_NO_ORIENT is absent from all public `_with_flags` doc comments. The flag collision is a consequence of this omission: future contributors see only '8 = full-res 16-bit' in the API docs with no hint that bit 8 also suppresses orientation.

title: All WASM error paths produce opaque JsError strings with no structured error codes, making programmatic error handling in JS fragile
description: Every `return Err(JsError::new(&format!(...)))` produces a free-form string. JS callers that want to distinguish decode failures (bad file) from validation failures (too large) from I/O failures must substring-match error messages, which breaks if the message text changes. A structured error type (e.g. an enum serialised to a `{code, message}` JS object, or at minimum a numeric error code prefix in the message string) would let JS callers handle categories robustly without fragile text matching. This is consistent with how libjxl's own error API works.
evidence: Verified that all error returns in the WASM public API are free-form `JsError::new(...)` strings with no error codes. JS consumers must substring-match on error.message to distinguish error categories — a fragile pattern that breaks on any message rewording.

title: decode_dng_raw dimension check casts usize img.width/height to u32 for MAX_DIM comparison, silently truncating on platforms where usize > u32
description: At lines 1735-1739, `(w as u32) > MAX_DIM` casts `w` (type `usize`, returned from `dng::decode_bytes`) to `u32`. On a 64-bit platform, if `w` is, say, `0x1_0000_0001`, the cast truncates to `1`, passing the check silently. The `validate_orf_structure` function avoids this by comparing `info.width` (which is `u32`) directly against `MAX_DIM: u32`. For DNG, `img.width` and `img.height` are `usize`; the comparison should use `w > MAX_DIM as usize` to avoid silent truncation. The same issue exists in `decode_cr2_raw` (lines 2080-2084).
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:1735: `(w as u32) > MAX_DIM`. On wasm32 usize==u32 so no issue; on native 64-bit builds used for benchmarks, a DNG with width > u32::MAX would truncate and pass the check. The correct comparison is `w > MAX_DIM as usize`.

title: OUT_FULL_16 and OUT_NO_ORIENT share the same bit value (8), making them mutually ambiguous
description: Two distinct constants are both defined as `8`: `OUT_FULL_16 = 8` (line 551) controls emission of a full-resolution RGB16 buffer, while `OUT_NO_ORIENT = 8` (line 556) suppresses apply_orientation. Any caller that passes flag `8` intending to request the full-16 buffer simultaneously disables orientation, and vice versa. The process_orf_impl code at line 743 uses `OUT_FULL_16` to gate the packed buffer and at line 800 uses `OUT_NO_ORIENT` to skip orientation — both test `output_flags & 8`. The public doc comment for `process_dng_with_flags` (line 2207) documents bit 8 as 'full RGB16 (M3)' with no mention of NO_ORIENT, so callers cannot know they are activating both behaviours simultaneously.
evidence: Duplicate of logic-1 / concurrency-1 / errors-5. Same collision verified at lib.rs:551 and 556.

title: DNG/CR2 ProcessResult uses denominator 1 for absent rational EXIF fields; ORF uses denominator 0 as the absence sentinel
description: The ORF path (process_orf_impl, line 843) sets absent rationals as `(0, 0)` with the comment 'JS checks den==0 before dividing'. The DNG/CR2 path (process_dng_impl, line 1929-1936) sets them to `(0, 1)` — a non-zero denominator. JS consumers that gate on `den == 0` to detect absence will compute 0/1 = 0 for DNG/CR2 files instead of treating the field as absent, leading to silently wrong EXIF display (e.g. showing focal length = 0mm, f/0 rather than 'unknown').
code_excerpt:
// ORF (line 843-848):
exposure_num: info.exposure.map(|(n, _)| n).unwrap_or(0),
exposure_den: info.exposure.map(|(_, d)| d).unwrap_or(0),  // 0 = absent
...
// DNG (line 1929-1935):
exposure_num: 0,
exposure_den: 1,  // NOT 0; JS sentinel broken
fnumber_num: 0,
fnumber_den: 1,
evidence: Duplicate of logic-5. Verified at lib.rs:841-848 and 1929-1935.

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: PerceptualComparer::new does not validate that ref_rgba length equals width*height*4
description: The constructor accepts `ref_rgba: &[u8]`, `width: usize`, and `height: usize` but performs no length check before passing them to `PerceptualCore::new`. If the JS caller passes mismatched dimensions (e.g. an empty slice with non-zero dims), the inner kernel will read out-of-bounds or produce silently wrong metrics. The scratch buffer is allocated from `width * height` without overflow check, so very large dims could also cause an OOM panic in WASM. By contrast, every other public WASM entry point in this file validates length and guards against overflow.
evidence: Duplicate of errors-1, errors-10, security-3. All confirm the same missing validation at lib.rs:2263-2267.

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: PerceptualComparer::all_at does not validate that len <= scratch buffer length, risking out-of-bounds slice
description: The method slices `&buf[..len]` where `len` is caller-supplied and `buf` is the scratch buffer. If `input_ptr` was never called, the scratch buffer has the size allocated at construction (`n * 4`). A JS caller may pass `len` larger than that (e.g. a frame with more pixels than the reference), causing a panic in the slice operation. The method has no guard and no documented contract on the maximum valid `len`.
evidence: Duplicate of errors-2. Verified at lib.rs:2298-2304.

title: frame_stats uses the 4-lane word-hash (not FNV) for full-buffer case but the public doc promises frameHashInt is 'the exact FNV-1a value'
description: The docstring at line 2806 says 'frameHashInt is the exact FNV-1a value — bit-identical to the shipped JS hash'. However, the full-buffer path (line 2823) calls `fs_core_simd`, which uses the 4-lane word-hash whose hash identity explicitly differs from byte-FNV. Only the truncated path calls `fs_core_trunc_word`, also not exact FNV. The production exact-FNV SIMD kernel `fs_core_simd_exact` exists but is NOT used in `frame_stats`. Consumers that log `frameHashInt` and compare across native/WASM or across the transition will observe mismatches despite the promise of bit-identity.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2806-2828. Line 2808 states 'bit-identical to the shipped JS hash' but line 2816 immediately corrects this: 'frameHashInt therefore differs from the JS FNV value (by design, post-audit)'. The stale contradictory doc at line 2808 would mislead any consumer who reads only the first sentence. The actual word-hash divergence from JS FNV is real and intentional, but the comment is self-contradicting.

title: take_rgb and rgb() are both public on ProcessResult but take_rgb empties the buffer; a caller invoking rgb() after take_rgb gets silently empty data
description: Both `take_rgb()` and `rgb()` are public wasm-bindgen methods that return the internal RGB8 buffer. `take_rgb` moves it out (returning empty on subsequent calls), while `rgb` always clones it. There is no documentation on either method warning that `take_rgb` invalidates subsequent `rgb()` or `take_rgba()` calls. JS callers that call `rgb()` first and `take_rgb()` second get inconsistent results silently, rather than an error. The `take_rgba` method (line 296) calls `rgb_to_rgba(&std::mem::take(&mut self.rgb))` and would similarly return an empty RGBA if called after `take_rgb`.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:259-272. `take_rgb` calls `std::mem::take` which leaves self.rgb empty. A subsequent `rgb()` call clones an empty Vec and returns empty data. No documentation warns callers about this invalidation behavior.

title: LookRenderer::new always delegates to new_with_options with apply_rotation=true but the wasm-bindgen constructor signature does not expose apply_rotation, leaving no way to construct the rotation-skipping variant from JS via new()
description: The `new` constructor is the wasm-bindgen `#[wasm_bindgen(constructor)]` and only calls `new_with_options(..., true)`. The `new_with_options` variant that takes `apply_rotation: bool` is exported but NOT marked `#[wasm_bindgen(constructor)]`, so it is callable from JS as a regular method — but JS `new LookRenderer(...)` always applies rotation. This is a capability exposure gap: the rotation-skipping fast path (documented as saving a full-buffer transpose per slider tick) cannot be accessed through the idiomatic JS constructor, making the optimisation invisible to consumers who discover the API via `new`.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:1453-1461. The `#[wasm_bindgen(constructor)]` always passes `apply_rotation=true`. `new_with_options` at line 1469 accepts the flag but is exported as a plain method, not a constructor — JS callers using `new LookRenderer(...)` have no way to opt into the rotation-skip fast path without knowing to call `LookRenderer.new_with_options(..., false)` instead.

title: rows_to_json hand-rolls JSON serialisation with no escaping of the filename field, allowing malformed JSON if a filename contains backslashes or quotes
description: The `file` field is written as `row.file.replace('\\', "/")` (line 206), which converts backslashes to forward slashes, but does not escape double-quotes, newlines, tabs, or other JSON-special characters. On Windows file systems, filenames can legally contain characters that would break JSON structure (e.g. a filename with `"` would terminate the JSON string prematurely). The resulting `benchmark/results_native.json` would be unparseable by downstream consumers that import it for comparison.
code_excerpt: out.push_str(&format!("      \"file\": \"{}\",\n", row.file.replace('\\', "/")));
evidence: Duplicate of security-4. Same unescaped interpolation verified at raw_decode_bench.rs:206.

title: bench_jxl_roundtrip assigns decode_region_downsample_ms = decode_ms (a JXL decode timing) to all three format paths, making it semantically wrong for the 'decode work for the requested region' field
description: The comment on `decode_region_downsample_ms` in BenchRow (line 177) states it is 'the decode work performed for the requested region'. The DNG, CR2 and ORF paths all set this to `decode_ms` — i.e. the JXL re-decode time of the full image, not a region sub-decode. This conflates full-decode cost with ROI decode cost in the handoff summary table printed at the end, breaking the 'apples-to-apples with WASM onMetric' contract stated in the doc comment at line 299.
evidence: Verified at raw_decode_bench.rs:299. `decode_region_downsample_ms` is set to `decode_ms` (the full JXL re-decode time from bench_jxl_roundtrip), while the field comment at line 175-176 defines it as 'the decode work performed for the requested region'. A full-image JXL decode is not a region decode.

category: no input validation at public boundary
title: fstats_prepare accepts arbitrary w and h without overflow or size guard, enabling OOM in WASM
description: The `fstats_prepare` function allocates `w * h * 4` bytes without checking for overflow or a maximum size. On a 32-bit WASM heap, passing `w=65536, h=65536` would overflow `usize` and produce a zero-length or tiny allocation, after which the LCG fill loop would write out-of-bounds. Even without overflow, a pathologically large value can OOM the tab. Every other public WASM function with size arguments uses `checked_mul` and an explicit pixel cap.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2337-2338. `let len = w * h * 4` uses two unchecked multiplies. On wasm32 overflow wraps len to a small value; LCG fill then writes the correct large iteration count into an undersized allocation — though Rust will bounds-check the `*slot = ...` write and panic before UB occurs. Still a crash from a bench-only API with no pixel cap.

category: no API schema / no contract testing
title: No versioning field or capability token on ProcessResult, making breaking additions invisible to callers who check only field presence
description: ProcessResult has grown 25+ fields over time (rgb, rgb16_lb, rgb16_thumb, rgb16_full, GPS, EXIF, look, perceptual, etc.). There is no `api_version` or `capabilities` field to allow JS callers to guard against forward-incompatible changes. As new optional outputs are added (e.g. OUT_FULL_16), callers consuming the struct from an older WASM bundle silently get zero-dim empty buffers with no way to distinguish 'feature not supported' from 'feature returned empty due to absent flag'. A version or feature-set bitmask in the struct would let JS code adapt gracefully.
evidence: Whether an api_version field is needed depends on the deployment model — if WASM and JS are always released together, the absence of versioning is an acceptable tradeoff. Cannot determine from the source alone whether callers ever run against mismatched WASM bundles.

title: fs_core_simd reads bytes twice: once via SIMD v128_load and again as individual bytes for the hash
description: In the wasm32 fs_core_simd inner loop, each chunk of 16 bytes is loaded as a v128 for the stats path, then the same 16 bytes are re-read individually via d[i..i+15] to build the four u32 words for the hash. This doubles memory traffic for the hash portion on the 4× most common code path. The four u32 words could be extracted from the already-loaded v128 using i32x4_extract_lane instead of four separate from_le_bytes calls.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2547-2554. The v128 for stats is loaded at line 2522 but not reused for the hash. Lines 2547-2550 re-read the same 16 bytes as four `from_le_bytes` calls. Using `i32x4_extract_lane` on the already-loaded v128 would eliminate these redundant byte loads.

title: fs_core_simd_exact inner byte loop defeats SIMD by processing hash serially inside the vectorised chunk loop
description: fs_core_simd_exact (the production kernel) loads each 16-byte chunk with v128_load for the stats, but then iterates a scalar for k in 0..16 loop inside the same chunk iteration to apply byte-wise FNV. This serial dependency chain of 16 successive XOR+MUL operations within each vectorised chunk is the bottleneck the comment attributes to the JS version. The SIMD stats gain is paid for by still running the same serial FNV that makes the JS slow. A word-level approximation (as used in fs_core_simd) or batched scalar extraction would break the chain.
evidence: Verified — fs_core_simd_exact is a bench-only export, not called by frame_stats production path. The finding is accurate: the serial FNV loop at lines 2657-2660 within the SIMD chunk loop eliminates any parallelism benefit for the hash component. Low production impact since fs_core_simd_exact is not the production path.

title: apply_look clones the full-resolution rgb16 buffer on every texture/clarity edit
description: When texture or clarity is nonzero, apply_look calls rgb16_src.to_vec() to produce a mutable copy before in-place sharpening. For a 20 MP image this is a ~120 MB allocation on the slider hot path. LookRenderer.render() has the same pattern at line 1586. The code comments acknowledge the issue for LookRenderer but it is unresolved for the apply_look path, which is the stateless (non-LookRenderer) call path. A possible mitigation is to provide a caller-owned scratch buffer or to limit the sharpening step to a downscaled proxy.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:786-788 (in process_orf_impl). `if params.texture != 0.0 || params.clarity != 0.0` triggers `apply_unsharp_masks(&mut rgb16, ...)` after borrowing `rgb16` mutably, requiring the clone via `to_vec()` on the slider hot path.

title: take_rgba allocates a new full-resolution RGBA buffer by copying from the rgb field instead of converting in-place
description: ProcessResult::take_rgba calls rgb_to_rgba on the result of std::mem::take(&mut self.rgb), which first moves the Vec<u8> out of the struct (OK), but rgb_to_rgba then allocates a brand-new Vec<u8> of 4/3 the size and copies into it, leaving the original buffer immediately dropped. For a 20 MP image the original rgb buffer is ~60 MB and the output is ~80 MB; both exist simultaneously. An in-place interleave (shifting bytes from the right) would halve peak memory use.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:296-303. `rgb_to_rgba` takes a `&[u8]` and allocates a new Vec with 4/3 size. The 3-byte-per-pixel input and 4-byte-per-pixel output both exist simultaneously during conversion, doubling peak usage vs. an in-place approach.

title: decode_orf_raw always runs full MHC demosaic AND planar-bilinear demosaic, paying double demosaic cost every call
description: decode_orf_raw runs demosaic_rggb_planar (for the fast preview path, lines 628-631) and then unconditionally also runs demosaic_rggb_mhc (for the full quality path, line 672). When only OUT_LIGHTBOX | OUT_THUMB is needed (no OUT_FULL_RGB8), the expensive MHC demosaic is wasted. The output_flags are passed to process_orf_impl, not to decode_orf_raw, so there is no early-exit. Splitting the decode function or passing flags through would avoid the redundant full-res MHC demosaic for preview-only callers.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:628 and 672. `decode_orf_raw` has no `output_flags` parameter. Both `demosaic_rggb_planar` (line 628) and `demosaic_rggb_mhc` (line 672) always run. The comment at line 624 says 'mhc only for full quality tone when OUT_FULL_RGB8' but the code contradicts this — MHC runs unconditionally.

title: rows_to_json builds JSON via repeated String::push_str with many format! allocations per row
description: rows_to_json constructs the JSON output by calling out.push_str(&format!(...)) for every field of every row. Each format! call allocates a temporary String that is immediately copied then dropped. For a 30-file Gobabeb run (30 rows × ~15 fields = ~450 format! allocations) this is harmless in absolute terms since it runs once, but it is structurally a string-concat-in-loop pattern. Using a serde_json serializer or a single write! to a BufWriter would eliminate all intermediate allocations. This is a benchmark tool, so severity is low.
evidence: Verified at raw_decode_bench.rs:194-228. Each field is formatted with a separate `format!()` call creating a temporary String. For a benchmark-only tool the impact is negligible, but the pattern is structurally redundant — a BufWriter+write! or serde_json would eliminate the intermediate allocations.

title: OUT_FULL_16 packs the full-resolution rgb16 into a new Vec<u8> before the rgb16 buffer is used for tone, causing a peak of three concurrent large buffers
description: process_orf_impl packs the full 16-bit buffer into rgb16_full (a new Vec<u8> ~6 bytes/pixel) at line 743-747 before the subsequent tonemapping step, which also reads rgb16 and emits rgb8 (~3 bytes/pixel). For a 20 MP image, rgb16 (~120 MB), rgb16_full (~120 MB), and rgb8 (~60 MB) all coexist. Reordering to pack rgb16_full after drop(rgb16) (which already occurs at line 800) would reduce peak by ~120 MB, or packing and dropping rgb16 before tone would save ~60 MB depending on path.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:743-748. `pack_rgb16_full(&rgb16, w, h)` allocates a new Vec<u8> of w*h*6 bytes (120 MB for 20 MP) while `rgb16` (w*h*6 bytes as u16, also ~120 MB) is still live. Then the tone path at line 791 allocates `rgb8` (~60 MB). All three buffers coexist at peak.

title: demosaic_bench_equal and demosaic_bench_first_diff both run two full demosaic passes each call, used from bench tooling but exported as always-available WASM API
description: demosaic_bench_equal (line 52) calls demosaic_rggb and demosaic_rggb_simd and compares the results, while demosaic_bench_first_diff (line 59) does the same. Each runs two full demosaic operations on the bench buffer. These are bench-only exports but they are compiled into every production WASM build (no #[cfg] guard), contributing to code-size and making them callable from any JS consumer at any time. Similar patterns exist for the planar variants (lines 97-118). Guarding with a compile-time feature flag would avoid the code-size cost in production.
evidence: Verified that demosaic_bench_equal and similar bench-only exports carry no `#[cfg(feature = "bench")]` guard and are compiled into every WASM build. This inflates code size and exposes double-demosaic APIs to any JS caller without need.

title: bench_jxl_encode_with_ch creates a new Encoder (and its internal thread pool) on every run iteration
description: The inner bench loop (0..RUNS) calls Encoder::with_threads on each iteration. Each Encoder construction likely initialises a thread pool or at least per-call metadata. The measured hot path is the encode call itself, but the allocation and setup of the Encoder leaks into timing if construction is non-trivial. Moving Encoder construction outside the timed region (before the loop) and reusing it across RUNS would give cleaner measurements and avoid unnecessary overhead.
evidence: Verified at raw_decode_bench.rs:82-106. `Encoder::with_threads(...)` is constructed inside `for _ in 0..RUNS`. The timing `Instant::now()` starts AFTER the constructor, so Encoder setup doesn't contaminate the timing measurement itself. The issue is wasted Encoder construction (thread pool init) on each run, not timing contamination — confirmed as a real performance waste.

title: pack_rgb16_full is a pure interleaved u16 → packed-LE-u8 loop with no SIMD; it runs on every OUT_FULL_16 request for full-res images
description: pack_rgb16_full iterates pixel-by-pixel over the full-resolution rgb16 buffer (potentially 20 MP = 60 M u16 reads → 120 M u8 writes) using a scalar loop with write_rgb16_le. On wasm32 this could use v128 loads of two pixels (6 u16 = 12 bytes = ~1 v128) and repack with i8x16_shuffle to produce the 6-byte LE output. On native x86 the compiler may auto-vectorise but the shuffle pattern is non-trivial and manual SIMD would guarantee it. Given this is the M3 16-bit export path (pyramid big levels), throughput matters.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:351-361. `pack_rgb16_full` uses a scalar pixel-by-pixel loop with no SIMD. It runs on every OUT_FULL_16 call — the M3 pyramid path. For a 20 MP image this is 60M u16 reads into 120M u8 writes with no vectorization applied manually.

title: Benchmark harness has no performance-regression CI integration; results are written to a local JSON file only
description: results_native.json is written to a local path (benchmark/results_native.json) with no automated comparison against a baseline or any CI step that would catch regressions. The harness already has the structure (generated_at, runsPerFile, rows with per-stage ms) to support a simple threshold check. Adding a CI job that runs the bench on a fixed corpus and compares key metrics (decompressMs, demosaicMs, tonemapMs) against a committed baseline JSON would catch perf regressions before merge.
evidence: Cannot verify from the source code alone whether a CI pipeline with benchmark regression detection exists. The bench output format supports it, but CI configuration is outside the repo view.

title: fs_core_trunc_word re-checks four branch conditions per pixel for the truncation guard, serialising what could be a single range check
description: fs_core_trunc_word issues four separate if i < limit / if i+1 < limit / if i+2 < limit / if i+3 < limit checks per pixel. For pixels well inside the limit (the common case for partial frames) these are predictable branches but still consume decode slots. A single if i + 3 < limit guard for the fast path, falling into scalar handling only for the boundary pixel, would reduce branch count by 3× for the bulk.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:2744-2774. Four separate limit checks per pixel where a single `if i + 3 < limit` guard for the bulk path would suffice. Only the tail pixel needs the four individual checks. This is a low-impact scalar kernel used only for partial/truncated frames.
